title: Defiance, chaos and repression at the CJP's Sansad March
author: Amrita Singh,Nikita Saxena,Puja Sen,Shahid Tantray
image_caption: Several people described the actions of the security forces as thuggish and vindictive but insisted that facing down the lathis and tear-gas shells had not dented their resolve.SHAHID TANTRAY FOR THE CARAVAN
content_html:
ON THE NIGHT of 19 July, several buses of the Delhi Police, some of them carrying barricades, flanked Sansad Marg and Tolstoy Marg to the mouth of the Jantar Mantar Road, where the Cockroach Janta Party had been protesting for the past month. Several thousand people were present at the protest site, with more streaming in, many of them carrying food and water. The air reverberated with chants of “Jai Bhim,” “Inqilab Zindabad,” “Vande Mataram” and “Bharat Mata ki Jai,” as well as slogans calling for the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan—and a few asking for Prime Minister Narendra Modi to do so too.

Shortly after midnight, we saw a few men at one of the entry points to the protest site, near a security check post. One of them was perched on top of two parallel rows of police barricades, using a sledgehammer to knock out the metal rods connecting them. When people asked them what they were doing, and why, they said something about the prospect of a police crackdown but did not elaborate. It was unclear whether they were from the police or protesters taking matters into their own hands. A bystander tried to figure out a way to alert the CJP leadership on the main stage but soon gave up—not only were there too many people in the way, it seemed impossible to get a message across.

Besides the CJP itself, several other organisations, including the All India Students’ Association and the Students’ Federation of India, had put up encampments in the protest site, each surrounded by throngs of cadre and supporters. The prevailing sense of confusion, as well as paranoia over how the Modi government would react to the protest, portended what was to come the following day, when over fifty thousand people arrived to march to parliament, only to be met by wanton state violence.

Over several hours on 20 July, in locations across central Delhi, police and paramilitary personnel attacked the protesters—who were, for the most part, peaceful—with lathi charges and tear gas. More than a hundred people were hospitalised with various injuries, and around seventy were detained. Some of the detainees told us that they had been beaten during the hours they spent in the Parliament Street police station, before being released that evening. By then, a detachment of security forces had made their way into Jantar Mantar, beaten protesters and dismantled most of the encampments at the site. Most of them removed the name badges on their uniforms to avoid identification. Throughout the day, mobile internet services were disrupted in the area, making it difficult to connect, coordinate or share information.
content_text:
ON THE NIGHT
of 19 July, several buses of the Delhi Police, some of them carrying barricades, flanked Sansad Marg and Tolstoy Marg to the mouth of the Jantar Mantar Road, where the Cockroach Janta Party had been protesting for the past month. Several thousand people were present at the protest site, with more streaming in, many of them carrying food and water. The air reverberated with chants of “Jai Bhim,” “Inqilab Zindabad,” “Vande Mataram” and “Bharat Mata ki Jai,” as well as slogans calling for the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan—and a few asking for Prime Minister Narendra Modi to do so too.
Shortly after midnight, we saw a few men at one of the entry points to the protest site, near a security check post. One of them was perched on top of two parallel rows of police barricades, using a sledgehammer to knock out the metal rods connecting them. When people asked them what they were doing, and why, they said something about the prospect of a police crackdown but did not elaborate. It was unclear whether they were from the police or protesters taking matters into their own hands. A bystander tried to figure out a way to alert the CJP leadership on the main stage but soon gave up—not only were there too many people in the way, it seemed impossible to get a message across.
Besides the CJP itself, several other organisations, including the All India Students’ Association and the Students’ Federation of India, had put up encampments in the protest site, each surrounded by throngs of cadre and supporters. The prevailing sense of confusion, as well as paranoia over how the Modi government would react to the protest, portended what was to come the following day, when over fifty thousand people arrived to march to parliament, only to be met by wanton state violence.
Over several hours on 20 July, in locations across central Delhi, police and paramilitary personnel attacked the protesters—who were, for the most part, peaceful—with lathi charges and tear gas. More than a hundred people were hospitalised with various injuries, and around seventy were detained. Some of the detainees told us that they had been beaten during the hours they spent in the Parliament Street police station, before being released that evening. By then, a detachment of security forces had made their way into Jantar Mantar, beaten protesters and dismantled most of the encampments at the site. Most of them removed the name badges on their uniforms to avoid identification. Throughout the day, mobile internet services were disrupted in the area, making it difficult to connect, coordinate or share information.

title: The bloody record of the Delhi Police
author: Prabhjit Singh
image_caption: The Delhi Police appears to have a habit of giving itself a clean chit, with no authority able to hold it to account.Srinivas Kuruganti
content_html:
TWO YEARS AFTER the Bharatiya Janata Party leader Kapil Mishra gave a threatening speech about protesters in northeastern Delhi, precipitating three days of communal violence, Yati Narsinghanand delivered yet another incendiary speech in the national capital. At a Hindu Mahapanchayat attended by around three hundred people, on 3 April 2022, Narsinghanand, the head of the Dasna Devi temple in Ghaziabad, claimed, “Once a Muslim becomes the prime minister of India, in twenty years, fifty percent of you will change your religion. Forty percent of Hindus will be murdered.”

Three months earlier, Narsinghanand had been arrested by the Uttarakhand Police for calling for a genocide of Muslims at a similar public meeting in Haridwar. Despite international outrage, he was released on bail within a month. His arrest did not dilute his vitriol. “If you want to change this future, become men,” he said at the Hindu Mahapanchayat in Delhi. “He who is armed is a man.”

Mishra’s speech, in February 2020, had been made in the presence of a senior Delhi Police official. There were at least thirty police personnel at the Mahapanchayat where Narsinghanand spoke, even though it was held without requisite permission. Despite live media coverage of the event, the Delhi Police did little to halt it, though the police did subsequently register first-information reports. This was among the many episodes over the past two years that have made it evident that the Delhi Police is doing little to stop the escalation of communal tensions in the capital.
content_text:
TWO YEARS AFTER
the Bharatiya Janata Party leader Kapil Mishra gave a threatening speech about protesters in northeastern Delhi, precipitating three days of communal violence, Yati Narsinghanand delivered yet another incendiary speech in the national capital. At a Hindu Mahapanchayat attended by around three hundred people, on 3 April 2022, Narsinghanand, the head of the Dasna Devi temple in Ghaziabad, claimed, “Once a Muslim becomes the prime minister of India, in twenty years, fifty percent of you will change your religion. Forty percent of Hindus will be murdered.”
Three months earlier, Narsinghanand had been arrested by the Uttarakhand Police for calling for a genocide of Muslims at a similar public meeting in Haridwar. Despite international outrage, he was released on bail within a month. His arrest did not dilute his vitriol. “If you want to change this future, become men,” he said at the Hindu Mahapanchayat in Delhi. “He who is armed is a man.”
Mishra’s speech, in February 2020, had been made in the presence of a senior Delhi Police official. There were at least thirty police personnel at the Mahapanchayat where Narsinghanand spoke, even though it was held without requisite permission. Despite live media coverage of the event, the Delhi Police did little to halt it, though the police did subsequently register first-information reports. This was among the many episodes over the past two years that have made it evident that the Delhi Police is doing little to stop the escalation of communal tensions in the capital.
